content.text: In
Claude.ai
, you prompt primarily in the
main chat input box
at the bottom of the conversation surface, and you can also use
project‑level instructions
and
built‑in prompting tools
to structure and refine prompts.
1. Main chat input (the core place to prompt)
Where it is:
On the
Claude.ai
web UI (or in the app), open a chat with Claude.
At the bottom of the screen, you see a text input field with a
Send
button (or an arrow icon).
What it does:
This is where you type your
user prompt
: the task, question, or instruction for Claude.
Everything you send here becomes part of the conversation context and is used by Claude to generate its response.
How to use it well:
Start with a clear
role + task + context + constraints + output format
Use line breaks, lists, and tags like
<context>
<examples>
<rules>
to structure your prompt.
For long tasks, you can paste data directly into this input or attach files (if your interface supports attachments).
2. Project / Conversation-level instructions (for reusable context)
Claude.ai
supports
Projects
(sometimes called “Claude Projects”) where you can define persistent context and instructions for a group of conversations.
Where it is:
In the left sidebar, under
Projects
(or a similar section).
Create a new project (e.g., “Prompt Rewriter”, “Marketing Assistant”, “Code Reviewer”).
Inside the project, there’s often an instructions field or a place to set
system-like guidance
for that project.
What it does:
You can write
project instructions
that act like a system prompt: define Claude’s role, style, rules, and typical workflows for that project.
Every conversation in that project inherits this context, so you don’t need to repeat the same instructions in every prompt.
Example:

You are an expert prompt engineer specializing in improving Claude prompts.

Rules:
- Always ask clarifying questions before rewriting.
- Keep rewritten prompts concise but explicit.
- Output the final prompt in a markdown block.
- Do not execute the prompt; just optimize it.
This is especially useful when you want a
consistent collaborator persona
across many prompts in a project.
3. Prompt Generator and Prompt Improver (built-in tools)
Claude Console /
Claude.ai
offers
prompting tools
to help you build and refine prompts:
“The Claude Console also offers prompting tools—prompt generator, templates and variables, and prompt improver—to help you build and refine prompts quickly.”
Prompt Generator
If you don’t have a first draft, you can use the
Prompt Generator
in the Claude Console.
It asks you questions about your task and then generates a structured prompt for you.
Where to find it:
In the Claude Console (or within
Claude.ai
’s tools panel), look for
Prompt Generator
or similar.
It may be an interactive tool or a button that opens a guided form.
Prompt Improver
If you already have a rough prompt, you can run it through the
Prompt Improver
It analyzes your prompt and suggests improvements: clarity, structure, examples, constraints, etc.
How to use:
Paste your draft prompt into the tool.
Let it suggest changes.
Then paste the improved prompt back into the main chat input to use with Claude.
4. Interactive Prompt Maker (artifact-based tool)
There is also an
Interactive Prompt Maker
artifact on
Claude.ai
“Create perfect AI prompts in 3 steps: describe your task, answer tailored questions, get optimized prompts. Built with Claude AI at
Claude.ai
How it works:
You describe your task.
The tool asks tailored questions (e.g., audience, tone, format, constraints).
It generates an optimized prompt for you.
Where to use:
In
Claude.ai
, you can open this as an interactive artifact (a guided tool).
The final prompt is then copied into the
main chat input
to use with Claude.
5. How the pieces fit together: where to “prompt” in practice
Typical workflow:
Start in the main chat input:
Type your initial task in the bottom input box and hit send.
If you want reusable context:
Create a
Project
and set persistent instructions there, then prompt inside that project.
If you don’t have a good prompt yet:
Use the
Prompt Generator
or
Interactive Prompt Maker
to build one.
Then paste the generated prompt into the main chat input.
If you have a rough prompt:
Use the
Prompt Improver
to refine it.
Then paste the improved prompt into the main chat input.
In short:
you type your actual prompts into the main chat input
; projects and tools help you design, refine, and reuse them.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of in claude.ai helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with in claude.ai has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Advanced Best Practices
Production experience with in claude.ai has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Prompt Input Modalities in Claude.ai

Claude.ai accepts input in several formats beyond plain text. Understanding all the input modalities available to you dramatically expands what you can accomplish without writing a single line of code. The chat interface supports plain text, formatted markdown (which Claude renders automatically in its responses), pasted code, and structured data. Beyond text, you can attach files — PDFs, Word documents, spreadsheets, images, and code files — directly in the chat interface by clicking the paperclip icon or dragging and dropping. When you attach a file, Claude receives its content as part of the context window. For images specifically, Claude uses its vision capabilities to read charts, screenshots, UI mockups, handwritten notes, and photos. You can also paste images directly into the input box in most browsers, making screenshot-driven workflows especially fast.
Text input: plain prose, markdown, code snippets, JSON, or any structured text pasted directly
File attachments: PDFs, .docx, .xlsx, .csv, .txt, .py, .js, .ts and most plain-text formats
Image attachments: PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP — ideal for screenshots, diagrams, charts, and mockups
Pasted images: drag a screenshot from your OS or paste from clipboard (Ctrl+V / Cmd+V)
Code files: Claude reads syntax, structure, and comments; attach entire files for debugging or review
Multiple files simultaneously: attach several files in one message; Claude reasons across all of them
Conversation Management Features

Claude.ai gives you several tools for managing conversations over time. Every conversation is saved automatically and appears in your sidebar history, searchable by keyword. You can rename any conversation by clicking on its title, making it easy to organize ongoing projects. Within a conversation, you can edit any of your previous messages — click the pencil icon on your message to revise it, which creates a branch from that point. This is one of the most powerful but underused features: instead of starting a new chat to try a different approach, you edit the original prompt and Claude regenerates from there, leaving your original response still visible in the branch history. The artifacts panel (right side of the screen) collects all structured outputs — code blocks, documents, and rendered apps — produced during the conversation, letting you review or copy them without scrolling through the entire chat.
Auto-save: every conversation is persisted; nothing is lost if you close the tab or browser
Rename conversations: click the conversation title to give it a memorable name for later retrieval
Branching via message edit: click the pencil on any user turn to fork the conversation from that point
Artifacts panel: right-side drawer collects all code, documents, and rendered outputs in one place
Conversation search: use the search bar in the sidebar to find past chats by keyword or topic
Share conversations: generate a shareable read-only link to a conversation for colleagues
Star / pin conversations: mark frequently-used chats so they appear at the top of the sidebar
The Projects Feature

Projects are persistent workspaces in Claude.ai that maintain context across multiple conversations. Unlike a regular conversation — which starts with a blank slate each time — a Project lets you write project-level instructions that Claude reads at the start of every conversation within that project. Think of project instructions as a persistent system prompt: you define Claude's role, your preferred output format, domain-specific context, or ongoing constraints once, and every chat inside the project automatically benefits from them. Projects also allow you to upload knowledge files — documents that Claude can reference throughout all conversations in that project without you having to paste them each time. This is ideal for ongoing work like building a product, researching a topic, or maintaining a writing style guide.

# Project Instructions Example: Technical Writing Assistant

You are a senior technical writer helping to produce developer documentation.

## Your role
- Write clear, accurate documentation for software engineers.
- Default to code-first explanations: show working examples, then explain them.
- Always use British English spelling.

## Output standards
- Use markdown with h2/h3 headings.
- Include a TL;DR at the top of any document longer than 300 words.
- Put all code in fenced code blocks with the correct language tag.
- Never use passive voice when active voice is possible.

## Project context
This project documents a Python REST API library. The library uses `httpx` for async requests.
Authentication is always via Bearer tokens. Base URL: https://api.example.com/v1.

Once your project instructions are set, every new conversation in the project starts with Claude already in the right mindset, with the right persona and constraints — you never have to repeat yourself. Project-level knowledge files work similarly: upload a codebase, a style guide, or a reference document once, and Claude can cite or apply it in any conversation without re-uploading.
Common Mistakes When Prompting in Claude.ai

Most users plateau in their Claude.ai effectiveness because of a small set of avoidable habits. The most common mistake is treating Claude like a search engine: sending a single vague question and expecting a definitive answer, then being disappointed when the answer is too general. Claude responds far better to context-rich prompts that explain the goal, the audience, the constraints, and the desired output format. The second most common mistake is not iterating: treating Claude's first response as final rather than as a draft to refine. Real power-users send follow-up messages like 'make the second section shorter', 'rewrite that code to use async/await', or 'can you give me three alternative versions?' The third mistake is ignoring the Projects feature for recurring workflows — copying and pasting the same context paragraph into every conversation is exactly the problem Projects solve.
Vague prompts: 'write me something about X' produces generic output; specify format, audience, length, and goal
One-shot mindset: treat Claude's first response as a draft, not a final answer — iterate explicitly
No output format specified: without format guidance, Claude guesses; always say 'give me a JSON object', 'use a table', etc.
Ignoring Projects for repeated work: copy-pasting the same system context into every chat wastes time and context window
Forgetting attachments: describing a document in text when you could just attach it adds tokens and loses fidelity
Not reading the full response: Claude often puts the most important caveats or alternatives at the end
Stopping after one message: the best outputs usually emerge after 2-3 back-and-forth turns of refinement
Prompting Tips for Power Users

Experienced Claude.ai users develop a set of habits that consistently produce better results. Use the Shift+Enter shortcut to insert newlines in your prompt without sending it, which lets you compose long, structured multi-paragraph prompts. For code-heavy work, use triple backtick fences even in your user messages so Claude knows to treat a block as code rather than prose. When you want Claude to produce output in a specific format, show it a small example first — one example is often worth hundreds of words of description. For long documents or codebases, break your request into explicit phases: first ask Claude to read and summarize, then in a follow-up message ask for specific changes. This prevents Claude from making changes without fully understanding the material. Finally, use the model selector to choose between Claude models: the Haiku-class models are fastest for quick tasks, while Sonnet and Opus excel at complex reasoning, long documents, and nuanced writing.
Shift+Enter for newlines: compose multi-paragraph prompts without accidentally sending mid-thought
Triple backticks in your own message: wrap code in ``` ``` ``` so Claude treats it as code, not prose
One-shot examples: show a sample input-output pair before your actual request — dramatically improves format adherence
Phase your requests: read → summarize → modify, rather than asking for the full transformation in one shot
Model selector: use Haiku for speed on simple tasks, Sonnet for balanced quality, Opus for maximum capability
Edit past messages to branch: revise your original prompt instead of starting a new chat to try a different approach
Project knowledge files: upload reference documents once so Claude can access them in every chat without re-uploading
Claude.ai vs API: When to Use Each

Knowing which interface to reach for is itself a prompting skill. Claude.ai is the right tool for exploratory, conversational, and one-off tasks — it offers the fastest path from idea to response with no setup. The API is the right tool when you need Claude inside your own application, when you need to process large batches of inputs programmatically, or when you need to pass a system prompt that the end user never sees. Projects in Claude.ai partially bridge this gap by providing persistent instructions, but they do not offer the full programmatic control of the API.
Claude.ai: best for one-off tasks, exploration, writing, research, and interactive back-and-forth conversations
Claude.ai Projects: best for recurring workflows where you want persistent context without writing code
API: best when embedding Claude in your own application, processing batches, or needing hidden system prompts
API: required for programmatic control — temperature, max_tokens, stop sequences, tool use, streaming
Claude.ai Artifacts: best for interactive outputs like rendered HTML, runnable code, or React components
API + Claude.ai hybrid: prototype in Claude.ai, then translate the prompt pattern into an API call for production

# Translating a Claude.ai workflow to the API
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# The 'project instructions' from Claude.ai become the system prompt in the API
system_prompt = """
You are a senior technical writer producing developer documentation.
Write clear, accurate content for software engineers.
Use code-first explanations, British English, and markdown formatting.
Always include a TL;DR at the top of documents longer than 300 words.
"""

# The 'chat input' becomes the user message
user_message = """
Write a short guide explaining HTTP status codes 200, 400, 401, 403, and 500.
Include a Python code example that checks each status code after an API call.
"""

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1500,
    system=system_prompt,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": user_message}
    ]
)

print(response.content[0].text)
